title: Array.push/pop simulation
description: The `push()` method adds one or more elements to the end of an array and returns the new length. The `pop()` method removes the **last** element from an array and returns that element. Together, they allow an array to act as a **Stack** (Last-In, First-Out).
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array and an element.
Push the element onto the array.
Pop an element from the array.
Push the element onto the array again.
Return the final array.
skeletonHint: arr.push(val) adds to the end; arr.pop() removes from the end.
patternExplanation: Push and Pop are O(1) operations that manage the end of the array.

title: Array.unshift/shift simulation
description: The `unshift()` method adds elements to the **beginning** of an array, and `shift()` removes the **first** element. These methods are typically slower than push/pop because they require re-indexing all subsequent elements.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array and an element.
Add the element to the beginning using `unshift()`.
Remove the first element using `shift()`.
Add the element to the beginning again.
Return the final array.
skeletonHint: unshift(val) adds to the front; shift() removes from the front.
patternExplanation: Shift and Unshift are O(n) operations because they modify the indices of all existing elements.

title: Array.slice basics
description: The `slice()` method returns a **shallow copy** of a portion of an array into a new array object. It takes two arguments: `start` (inclusive) and `end` (exclusive). The original array is **not** modified.
whatShouldHappen: Return a slice of the array from index 1 to index 3 (not including index 3).
Return a second slice containing the last two elements of the array.
Return both slices in an array `[slice1, slice2]`.
skeletonHint: arr.slice(start, end) stops before the end index. arr.slice(-n) gets the last n items.
patternExplanation: Slice is a non-mutating way to extract data from an array.

title: Array.splice to remove element
description: The `splice()` method changes the contents of an array by removing or replacing existing elements. Unlike `slice()`, `splice()` **mutates** the original array.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array and an index.
Use `splice()` to remove 1 element at the given index.
Return the modified array.
skeletonHint: Syntax: arr.splice(start, deleteCount)
patternExplanation: Splice is the primary tool for in-place array modification.

title: Array.splice to insert element
description: By setting the `deleteCount` to `0`, `splice()` can be used to insert elements at any position without removing existing ones.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array, an index, and an element.
Use `splice()` to insert 'element' at 'index' without deleting anything.
Return the modified array.
skeletonHint: The third argument to splice is the item to add.
patternExplanation: Splice allows flexible insertion at any point in the array.

title: Array.indexOf vs findIndex
description: `indexOf` is great for simple values, but `findIndex` allows for complex conditions using a callback function.
whatShouldHappen: Find the index of 'search' using `indexOf`.
Find the index of the first string that starts with 'search' using `findIndex`.
Return both as [index1, index2].
skeletonHint: item.startsWith(search) can be used inside findIndex.
patternExplanation: indexOf is for exact matches; findIndex is for predicate-based searches.

title: Array.includes with objects
description: `Array.includes()` uses referential equality for objects. Two objects with the same properties are NOT considered equal unless they point to the same memory location.
whatShouldHappen: Check if 'arr' includes the object 'obj' (the reference).
Check if 'arr' includes a new object `{ name: obj.name }`.
Return [result1, result2].
skeletonHint: Objects are compared by reference, not by content.
patternExplanation: includes() fails for 'equal' objects that have different references.

title: Array.reverse in-place
description: The `reverse()` method reverses an array in-place. This means it modifies the original array and returns a reference to it.
whatShouldHappen: Reverse the input array 'arr'.
Return the reversed array.
skeletonHint: arr.reverse() modifies the original array.
patternExplanation: Reversing an array in-place is O(n) and saves memory compared to creating a copy.

title: Array.sort (numeric)
description: By default, `sort()` converts elements to strings and sorts them lexicographically. To sort numbers correctly, you must provide a compare function.
whatShouldHappen: Sort the array of numbers in ascending order.
Return the sorted array.
skeletonHint: Use a compare function: (a, b) => a - b
patternExplanation: The compare function defines the sort order based on return value (negative, zero, or positive).

title: Array.concat vs Spread
description: Both `concat()` and the spread operator (`...`) can be used to merge arrays into a new one without modifying the originals.
whatShouldHappen: Combine 'arr1' and 'arr2' using `concat()`.
Combine 'arr1' and 'arr2' using the spread operator in a new array literal.
Return [res1, res2].
skeletonHint: Spread syntax: [...arr1, ...arr2]
patternExplanation: Merging arrays creates a new array while preserving the original inputs.

title: Array.join custom delimiter
description: The `join()` method creates and returns a new string by concatenating all of the elements in an array, separated by a specified separator string. If no separator is provided, it defaults to a comma.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of strings and a delimiter.
Join the array elements using the delimiter.
Return the resulting string.
skeletonHint: Syntax: arr.join(separator)
patternExplanation: Join is the standard way to convert an array into a single formatted string.

title: Array.forEach basic
description: The `forEach()` method executes a provided function once for each array element. Unlike `map()`, it returns `undefined` and is typically used for side effects (like updating an external variable).
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of numbers.
Initialize a `sum` variable to 0.
Use `forEach()` to add each number to `sum`.
Return the total sum.
skeletonHint: arr.forEach(element => { ... })
patternExplanation: forEach provides a clean, declarative way to iterate over all elements.

title: Array.map transformation
description: The `map()` method creates a **new** array populated with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the calling array. It does not modify the original array.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of numbers.
Use `map()` to create a new array where each element is multiplied by 2.
Return the new array.
skeletonHint: arr.map(x => x * 2)
patternExplanation: Map is the foundation of functional programming in JS, allowing for immutable transformations.

title: Array.filter even numbers
description: The `filter()` method creates a **shallow copy** of a portion of an array, containing only the elements that pass the test implemented by the provided function.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of numbers.
Use `filter()` to extract only the even numbers.
Return the new array.
skeletonHint: Use the modulo operator (%) to check for evenness.
patternExplanation: Filter allows you to selectively extract data based on specific criteria.

title: Array.reduce sum
description: The `reduce()` method executes a user-supplied 'reducer' callback function on each element of the array, passing in the return value from the calculation on the preceding element. It reduces the entire array into a **single value**.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of numbers.
Use `reduce()` to sum all numbers in the array.
Provide `0` as the initial value.
Return the final sum.
skeletonHint: arr.reduce((accumulator, currentValue) => { ... }, initialValue)
patternExplanation: Reduce is a powerful tool for aggregating array data into a single output.

title: Array.reduce to object map
description: The `reduce()` method can be used to transform an array into an object. This is common when you want to create a lookup map (dictionary) from an array of objects for O(1) access by ID.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of user objects (each having an `id` and `name`).
Use `reduce()` to create an object where keys are the user IDs and values are the full user objects.
Provide an empty object `{}` as the initial value.
Return the resulting map.
skeletonHint: Return the accumulator (acc) in every iteration of reduce.
patternExplanation: Mapping arrays to objects is a key performance optimization for frequent data lookups.

title: Array.every validation
description: The `every()` method tests whether **all** elements in the array pass the test implemented by the provided function. It returns a Boolean value.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of numbers.
Use `every()` to check if every number is greater than 0.
Return the boolean result.
skeletonHint: every() stops as soon as it finds one element that fails the test.
patternExplanation: Every is used for universal quantification (checking if a rule applies to everything).

title: Array.some check
description: The `some()` method tests whether **at least one** element in the array passes the test implemented by the provided function. It returns true if it finds an element for which the provided function returns true.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of user objects (with a `role` property).
Use `some()` to check if at least one user has the role 'admin'.
Return the boolean result.
skeletonHint: some() stops as soon as it finds one element that passes the test.
patternExplanation: Some is used for existential quantification (checking if at least one item exists).

title: Array.find match
description: The `find()` method returns the **first element** in the provided array that satisfies the provided testing function. If no values satisfy the testing function, `undefined` is returned.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of user objects.
Use `find()` to retrieve the first user object where `role === 'admin'`.
Return the user object (or undefined).
skeletonHint: find() returns the value itself, not its index.
patternExplanation: Find is perfect for retrieving a specific record from a collection based on a unique property.

title: Array.fill specific value
description: The `fill()` method changes all elements in an array to a static value, from a start index to an end index. It is often used with `Array(n)` to initialize an array of size `n` with a default value.
whatShouldHappen: Create a new array of size 'n'.
Use `fill()` to populate the entire array with the value 'val'.
Return the new array.
skeletonHint: Array(n) creates an empty array of length n.
patternExplanation: Fill is a fast way to pre-allocate and initialize an array with a constant value.

title: Array.from with Set
description: The `Array.from()` static method creates a new Array instance from an iterable or array-like object. A very common pattern is to use it with `Set` to remove duplicates from an array and convert it back.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array with potential duplicates.
Create a `new Set` from the array to extract unique values.
Use `Array.from()` to convert that `Set` back into an array.
Return the unique array.
skeletonHint: Array.from(iterable) is the official way to convert Sets to Arrays.
patternExplanation: Combining Set and Array.from is the most concise way to get unique elements in JS.

title: Array.flat (level 1)
description: The `flat()` method creates a new array with all sub-array elements concatenated into it recursively up to the specified depth. By default, it flattens one level deep.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives a nested array.
Use `flat()` to flatten it by 1 level.
Return the flattened array.
skeletonHint: arr.flat() is equivalent to arr.flat(1).
patternExplanation: Flat simplifies nested array structures into a more linear form.

title: Array.flatMap usage
description: The `flatMap()` method returns a new array formed by applying a given callback function to each element of the array, and then flattening the result by one level. It is identical to a `map()` followed by a `flat(1)`.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of sentences.
Use `flatMap()` to split each sentence into words (using space as delimiter).
Return a single array containing all words.
skeletonHint: s.split(' ') returns an array of words.
patternExplanation: flatMap is efficient for operations that produce an array for each element and need a combined result.

title: Array.keys iteration
description: The `keys()` method returns a new **array iterator** object that contains the keys for each index in the array. You can convert this iterator into an array using the spread operator or `Array.from()`.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array.
Get the iterator from `arr.keys()`.
Convert the iterator to an array and return it.
skeletonHint: [...arr.keys()] is a quick way to get all indices of an array.
patternExplanation: Keys provides access to the structural indices of the array independently of its values.

title: Array.entries iteration
description: The `entries()` method returns a new array iterator object that contains key/value pairs for each index in the array as `[index, value]` arrays.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array.
Get the iterator from `arr.entries()`.
Convert the iterator to an array and return it.
skeletonHint: Each entry is a [key, value] pair.
patternExplanation: Entries allows for simultaneous access to both index and value during iteration.

title: Multidimensional array access
description: Accessing elements in multidimensional arrays (arrays of arrays) requires multiple sets of brackets `[][]`. Each set of brackets moves one level deeper into the structure.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives a 2D array, a row index, and a column index.
Access the element at `arr[row][col]`.
Return the element.
skeletonHint: arr[row] gets the inner array; [col] gets the item from it.
patternExplanation: 2D arrays are simply arrays where each element is itself an array. Row-major order is standard in JS.

title: Sum of diagonal in 2D array
description: In a square matrix, the main diagonal consists of elements where the row index equals the column index (`arr[i][i]`).
whatShouldHappen: The function receives a square 2D array.
Calculate the sum of the elements on the main diagonal.
Return the sum.
skeletonHint: A single loop can access the diagonal by using the same index for both dimensions.
patternExplanation: Main diagonal elements share the same index for both dimensions.

title: Transpose a matrix
description: Transposing a matrix means swapping its rows and columns. The element at `[i][j]` moves to `[j][i]`.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives a 2D array.
Create a new 2D array that is the transpose of the input.
Return the new array.
skeletonHint: Initialize the result matrix with 'cols' rows and 'rows' columns.
patternExplanation: Transposition flips a matrix over its diagonal.

title: Remove duplicates from array
description: Removing duplicates can be done using `filter` and `indexOf`. An element is a duplicate if its current index is not the same as its first occurrence index.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array.
Use `filter` to return a new array with all duplicates removed.
Return the new array.
skeletonHint: indexOf always returns the first index where a value is found.
patternExplanation: indexOf always returns the first occurrence, so if index !== indexOf(item), it's a duplicate.

title: Intersection of two arrays
description: The intersection of two arrays contains only the elements that are present in both arrays.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives two arrays.
Use `filter` and `includes` to find common elements.
Ensure the result has no duplicates.
Return the intersection.
skeletonHint: Filter elements from arr1 that exist in arr2, then unique them.
patternExplanation: Intersection finds the commonality between two collections.

title: Difference between two arrays
description: The difference of two arrays consists of elements that are in the first array but not in the second. This is a common set operation.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives two arrays, 'arr1' and 'arr2'.
Use `filter()` on 'arr1' to keep only the elements that are NOT in 'arr2'.
Use `includes()` to check for existence in 'arr2'.
Return the resulting array.
skeletonHint: arr1.filter(x => !arr2.includes(x))
patternExplanation: Difference identifies unique elements in the source collection that don't exist in the target.

title: Chunk an array into N sizes
description: Chunking an array means splitting it into multiple smaller arrays of a specified size. This is useful for pagination or processing data in batches.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array 'arr' and a number 'size'.
Create an empty array to hold the chunks.
Loop through the array, incrementing by 'size' each time.
Use `slice()` to extract each chunk and push it to the results.
Return the array of chunks.
skeletonHint: Use a for loop where the increment is 'size'.
patternExplanation: Chunking transforms a flat list into a nested structure for batched processing.

title: Shuffle an array (Fisher-Yates)
description: The Fisher-Yates (or Knuth) shuffle is an O(n) algorithm for generating a random permutation of a finite sequence. It works by iterating backwards through the array and swapping each element with a randomly selected one before it.
whatShouldHappen: Iterate from the last index down to the first index.
Pick a random index `j` such that `0 <= j <= i`.
Swap the elements at index `i` and index `j`.
Return the mutated array.
skeletonHint: Use destructuring assignment for a clean swap: [a, b] = [b, a].
patternExplanation: Fisher-Yates ensures every permutation is equally likely by choosing from the remaining unshuffled elements.

title: Rotate an array by K steps
description: Rotating an array to the right by `k` steps means that elements at the end move to the beginning. For example, rotating `[1, 2, 3]` by 1 step results in `[3, 1, 2]`.
whatShouldHappen: Normalize `k` using `k % arr.length` to handle cases where `k > length`.
Remove the last `k` elements from the array.
Add those elements to the front of the array.
Return the modified array.
skeletonHint: arr.splice(-k) removes and returns the last k elements.
patternExplanation: Rotation can be performed in-place using splice/unshift or by reversing sub-sections.

title: Flatten nested array (recursive)
description: Flattening a nested array into a single-level array can be achieved recursively. For each element, if it is an array, flatten it; otherwise, keep it.
whatShouldHappen: Use `reduce()` to iterate through the array.
If the current element is an array (check with `Array.isArray()`), call the function recursively.
If it is not an array, concat it to the accumulator.
Return the final flattened array.
skeletonHint: acc.concat() can take either a single value or an array.
patternExplanation: Recursive flattening allows handling arrays with arbitrary levels of nesting.

title: Find second largest number
description: To find the second largest number, you first need to remove duplicates and then find the second highest value. This can be done by sorting the unique values in descending order.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of numbers.
Remove duplicates from the array.
If there are fewer than 2 unique numbers, return `null`.
Sort the unique numbers in descending order.
Return the second element (index 1).
skeletonHint: Use new Set(arr) to easily remove duplicates.
patternExplanation: Finding rank-based statistics often requires deduplication and sorting.

title: Move all zeros to end
description: Moving elements to the end while preserving order is a common task. You can achieve this by filtering the non-zero elements and the zero elements separately, then combining them.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array 'arr'.
Filter all elements that are not zero.
Filter all elements that are zero.
Combine them so non-zeros come first.
Return the new array.
skeletonHint: The spread operator (...) is useful for combining arrays.
patternExplanation: Partitioning allows you to reorder elements based on a predicate while maintaining stability.

title: Array of objects sorting
description: When sorting arrays of objects, your compare function should access the specific property you want to sort by.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of user objects.
Sort the users by the `age` property in ascending order.
Return the sorted array.
skeletonHint: The compare function (a, b) => a.age - b.age works for ascending numbers.
patternExplanation: Accessing nested properties in a sort callback is standard for ordering data collections.

title: Group by category (Reduce)
description: Grouping data into categories is a classic use case for `reduce()`. You build an object where each key is a category name and each value is an array of items belonging to it.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array of items (each with a `category` property).
Use `reduce()` with an initial empty object `{}`.
For each item, if its category doesn't exist in the accumulator, create an empty array.
Push the item into its respective category array.
Return the accumulator object.
skeletonHint: Don't forget to return 'acc' at the end of the reduce callback.
patternExplanation: Reducing an array to a grouped object is essential for data organization and visualization.

title: Last index of element
description: While `indexOf()` searches from the beginning, `lastIndexOf()` searches from the end of the array, returning the index of the last occurrence of a value.
whatShouldHappen: The function receives an array 'arr' and a 'target' value.
Use `lastIndexOf()` to find the last position of 'target'.
Return the index.
skeletonHint: lastIndexOf returns -1 if the value is not found.
patternExplanation: Searching from the end is useful when you need the most recent occurrence of an item.
